finding: A device-level meter result becomes materially more attributable to the browser run when it matches a fresh, randomized time-frequency pulse sequence rather than merely detecting sound during a broad time window.

primary_sources.status: W3C First Public Working Draft, 5 November 2024
primary_sources.does_not_support: physical speaker emission or listener perception

primary_sources.supports: browser generation of fresh random bytes through getRandomValues for production challenge construction
primary_sources.does_not_support: truth of an acoustic detection claim

design_implication: Replace the single diagnostic pulse with a fresh six-pulse challenge whose timing and frequencies are recorded beside browser render evidence. Score an external meter trace against the exact challenge before calling device-level evidence correlated.
evidence_strength: implementation-ready prototype with deterministic unit tests; no live phone or microphone trace
uncertainty: A nearby source could intentionally reproduce the same challenge.
Unsynchronized clocks and coarse frequency detection can create false negatives.
A correlated device signal does not prove that a person heard the sound.
falsification_route: Generate a new challenge for each run. Reject browser attribution when the external detector cannot match at least 80 percent of the expected pulse centers within the declared timing, frequency, and SNR tolerances.

verification.method: node --test acoustic-challenge.test.mjs in an isolated local runtime using byte-identical source and test content before commit
cases: new entropy produces a distinct challenge

claim_limits: The scheduler specifies intended browser output but does not prove speaker emission.
Sequence correlation is corroborating device-level evidence, not proof of perception.
No deployment or iPhone runtime verification occurred in this run.
